topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door remained stubbornly shut. Liam stood in the hallway, his knuckles white where he gripped the brass knob, the silence of the empty office pressing in on him like physical weight. He’d been expecting a final word, a simple acknowledgment of the end of an era, something from Marcus, his mentor, the man who had shaped his entire career. Instead, there was only the faint, lingering scent of old paper and dust. Liam pushed the door open slowly, the sound of the latch clicking echoing too loudly in the cavernous space. Marcus’s desk was bare, save for a single, pristine leather-bound journal lying open to a blank page. A cold, sharp tension coiled in Liam’s stomach, tightening until he felt breathless. He walked to the window, staring out at the indifferent cityscape, his jaw tight enough to crack. "This isn't right," he muttered, the sound rough in his throat. "Where is he?"

[story 2]

Sarah reread the final email, the subject line a polite notification about a planned departure date, devoid of any warmth or farewell. Her fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling slightly. She felt a sudden, hot surge of indignation rising in her chest, making her breath catch. She had spent five years under David’s tutelage, pouring every ounce of her ambition into his guidance, and this abrupt silence was a profound insult. Sarah slammed her laptop shut, the sharp crack sounding jarring in the quiet apartment. She paced the length of the living room three times, her movements jerky and agitated. "He couldn't even send a note?" she whispered to the empty room, her voice laced with disbelief. The injustice of it burned behind her eyes, a fierce, hot pressure demanding an explanation that simply wasn't forthcoming.

[story 3]

When Amelia finally found the office door unlocked, she didn't bother knocking. She strode in, her boots echoing on the polished floor, her expression a mask of stiff disapproval. Everything was meticulously tidy, almost sterile, which only amplified the emptiness. She scanned the room, her eyes darting from the empty chair to the neatly stacked files, searching for any sign of the man she depended on. There was nothing. Amelia stopped dead center, her hands balling into fists at her sides. A low, vibrating hum started in her chest, a deep sense of being profoundly wronged washing over her. "This is unacceptable," she stated, her voice dangerously level, though the tremor in her hands betrayed the turmoil churning beneath her composure. "After everything he did for me, this is the only response I get?"
